title: Unit 1 — Introduction to AI
sub: Definition · History · Intelligent Agents · PEAS · Agentic AI

h: Definition of AI — four views (think/act humanly/rationally)
Intelligent agents — types (simple reflex, model-based, goal-based, utility-based, learning)
PEAS framework — apply to vacuum cleaner, chess player, medical diagnosis
Turing Test and Reverse Turing Test — importance and critique
AI applications in different domains (medical, robotics, NLP, gaming)
m: History of AI — key milestones and chronological development
Human intelligence vs Machine intelligence — comparison
AI tree — branches and interdisciplinary nature
Rational agent definition and rationality
Agentic AI — concept and significance
l: Neuroscience and cognition — how brain inspires AI
Importance of knowledge and learning in AI

qs.t: How do you define Artificial Intelligence (AI)? Explain AI applications in different areas as problem solving tool.
Define Artificial Intelligence (AI). When is a machine said to have passed the Turing Test? Discuss two fields of application of AI.
Can machine think? Explain your stance with appropriate reasons. Relate it with Turing Test and Reverse Turing Test.
Define Artificial Intelligence (AI). Differentiate between Natural (Human) Intelligence and Artificial Intelligence. Can AI replace Humans? Justify.
Define Artificial Intelligence (AI)? Differentiate between Natural (Human) Intelligence and Artificial Intelligence.
Define AI. What is the importance of Turning Test in AI? List applications of AI.
What is an Artificial Intelligence (AI)? Explain any two applications of AI in real field.
Define Artificial Intelligence (AI). Describe the importance and practical application of AI.
Define Artificial Intelligence. Justify that "system that think rationally and act rationally" is part of Artificial Intelligence.
What is artificial intelligence (AI)? Explain the Turing and its importance in artificial intelligence.
Discuss any two fields of your daily life where artificial intelligence has been applied.
What is an intelligent agent? How does learning agent work?
What are intelligent agents and how can we design intelligent agent? Explain with examples on relevance to PEAS framework.
What is a rational agent? "System that think like humans" and "System that act like humans" are the part of artificial intelligence. Justify these statement with practical example.
What is Artificial Intelligence (AI)? Discuss history of AI in brief.
Define AI. What is the importance of Turning Test in AI? List applications of AI.
Distinguish between knowledge and learning. What does acting humanly refer to? Explain. Define well defined problems.

title: Unit 2 — Problem Solving and Search
sub: Well-defined problems · CSP · BFS · DFS · A* · Minimax · Hill Climbing · Genetic Algorithm

h: A* algorithm — apply on graph with heuristic values (exam graph questions)
Minimax algorithm with alpha-beta pruning — apply on given tree
Crypto-arithmetic problems as CSP — SEND+MORE=MONEY, SWIM+WEAR=RELAX, LOGIC+LOGIC=PROLOG, WRONG+WRONG=RIGHT, TWO+TWO=FOUR
BFS vs DFS — compare with time/space complexity
Hill climbing — algorithm, problems (local maxima, plateau, ridge)
m: Greedy best-first search vs A* — why A* overcomes greedy's problems
Constraint satisfaction — node/path consistency, backtracking
Iterative deepening DFS
Simulated annealing — importance over hill climbing
Water jug problem / River crossing problem as production system
l: Formal problem definition — states, actions, transitions
Genetic algorithm — steps, operators (selection, crossover, mutation)

qs.t: Explain steps of problem solving with suitable example. Solve the following crypto-arithmetic algorithm SWIM + WEAR = RELAX.
Explain A* algorithm in following figure where starting node is S and destination is G.
qs.expr:
Graph: S(h=17)→A(h=10,edge=6), S→B(h=13,edge=5), S→C(h=10,edge=10) ... Destination G(h=0)
Node h-values: A=10,B=13,C=10,D=10,E=?,F=1,G=0

qs.t: What do mean by Constraints Satisfaction Problem? List all the constraints and solve the following crypto-arithmetic problem: EAT + THAT = APPLE.

How A* search overcomes problem associated with Greedy Best First Search? Using following figure and table where starting node is S and destination is G, compare the result of A* algorithm with greedy search.
What is Constrain Satisfaction problem? Solve the following problem LOGIC + LOGIC = PROLOG.
What is minmax search? Apply minmax search along with alpha-beta pruning for the following tree at max node at top.
What is well defined problem? Solve the following crypto-arithmetic problem: TWO + TWO = FOUR.
Why is searching important in problem solving? What are the drawbacks of greedy best-first search and how A* search technique is used to solve it. Explain with example.
Solve following crypto-arithmetic problem: SEND + MORE = MONEY. Assign different decimal digit to different letters. Explain the steps followed for the solution.
Discuss about alpha-beta pruning algorithm. Find the value of min max value using this concept in the following tree.
What do you understand by Production system problem? Solve the following crypto arithmetic problem: WRONG + WRONG = RIGHT.
List out the disadvantages of MIN-MAX algorithm for game playing and explain how Alpha-beta pruning helps to overcome the limitation of MIN-MAX algorithm with an example.
What do you understand by Constraint satisfaction problem? Solve the following crypto-arithmetic problem: SWIM + WEAR = RELAX.
Why Searching is important in problem solving? What do the drawbacks of greedy best-first search and how A* search algorithm solve it. Explain with an example.
What is blind search? Explain depth first search algorithm with an example and compare it with breadth first search.
Discuss the hill climbing search algorithm along with problem associated with it and discuss their solutions. Why simulated annealing is important?
Discuss the hill climbing search algorithm along with problems associated with it and discuss their solutions.
What is problem space? Solve the following crypto arithmetic problem by showing all the steps: BASE + BALL = GAMES.
What is searching? Explain Breadth First Search and Depth First Search and compare their performance criteria.
Discuss about the evaluation criteria for search algorithm. State the problems in hill climbing search algorithm.
What is the advantage of depth limit search? Compare it with other search strategies.
Why searching is necessary in AI? Explain about the role of production system with suitable example.

A farmer has a goat, a wolf and a cabbage on the west side of a river. He wants to get all of his animals and his cabbage across the river onto the east side. Formulate this puzzle as search, solve using search (any method), draw the search tree.
Devise an example to show how A* algorithm uses path cost and heuristic cost to generate best solution.
What is well defined problem? You are given two jugs, a 5-gallon one and a 7-gallon one... How can you get exactly 4 gallons of water in the 1-gallon jug? Solve the problem using production system.
Distinguish between Breadth First Search and Depth First Search. Write algorithm for Min Max algorithm with appropriate example of your own.

title: Unit 3 — Knowledge Representation & Probabilistic Reasoning
sub: Logic · CNF · Resolution · Semantic Net · Frames · Bayes · Fuzzy Logic

h: CNF conversion steps — all 7 steps with example (∀x loves(x,animals)→loved(x,someone))
Resolution Refutation — Santa/Reindeer/Scrooge, Colonel West, Curiosity killed the cat, John likes food
Semantic Network — build from given sentences (Tom the cat, cricket player, circus elephants)
Bayes' Theorem numericals — genetic defect, flu/measles, university height problem
Fuzzy Logic — membership functions, fuzzy inference system, Mamdani method
m: Frames vs Semantic Networks — differences, advantages, limitations
FOPL representation — converting English to predicate logic
Forward chaining vs Backward chaining — comparison with examples
Belief network / Bayesian network — structure and reasoning
Rules of inference — modus ponens, resolution, unification
l: Conceptual dependency — primitives and representation using scripts

qs.t: Assume the following facts: Every child loves Santa. Everyone who loves Santa loves any reindeer. Rudolph is a reindeer, and Rudolph has a red nose. Anything which has a red nose is weird or is a clown. No reindeer is a clown. Scrooge does not love anything which is weird. Using Resolution Refutation method derive the following conclusion: Scrooge is not a child.
What is CNF? Explain rule of inference along with example.

Define term semantic net. Represent the following sentences using semantic net representation: Tom is a cat. Tom caught a bird. Tom is owned by Sudeep. Tom is ginger in color. Cats like cream. The cat sat on the mat. A cat is a mammal. A bird is an animal. All mammals are animals. Mammals have fur. Sudeep age is 20.

List down the steps for converting to CNF and proof by resolution refutation. Convert the following sentences into FOPL and answer "Did Curiosity kill the cat" by resolution refutation: (a) Everyone who loves all animals is loved by someone. (b) Anyone who kills an animal is loved by no one. (c) Jack loves all animals. (d) Either Jack or Curiosity killed the cat.

Discuss about the different approaches knowledge representation. How do you make choice among these approaches? Is it possible to evaluate the knowledge representation? List down any issues associated with it.
Why CNF is necessary? "Everyone who loves all animals are loved by someone" represent this statement in FOPL and explain all the steps involved to convert it into CNF.

What is semantic network? Convert the following sentences into semantic network: (i) The avg height of an adult is 5'6". (ii) Cricket player is an adult male. (iii) Adult male is a person. (iv) Batting average of a cricket player is 37.59. (v) Rohit Paudel is a Fielder. (vi) Fielder is a cricket player. (vii) Team of Rohit Paudel is Nepal.
What is belief network? How reasoning is done in this network?

Define horn clause with example. Consider the following axioms: (i) Every child love Santa. (ii) everyone who loves Santa loves any reindeer. (iii) Rudolph is a reindeer, and Rudolph has a red nose. (iv) Anything which has a red nose is weird or is a clown. (v) No reindeer is a clown. (vi) Scrooge does not love anything which is weird. Represent these axioms in a predicate calculus and then convert each formula to CNF. List down the rules of inference.

It is a crime for an American to sell weapons to hostile nations. Nono has some missiles. All the missiles owned by Nono were sold to it by Colonel West. Missiles are weapons. An enemy of America counts as hostile. Colonel West is an American. The country Nono is an enemy of America. Prove that Colonel West is a criminal by using FOPL based Resolution Refutation Method.

List down the rule for Inference. Consider the following axioms: All hounds howl at night. Anyone who has any cats will not have any mice. Light sleepers do not have any thing which howls at night. John has either a cat or a hound. Prove: "If John is a light sleeper, then John does not have any mice." By using resolution refutation.
Explain frame and Semantic Net with example. List down their advantages and disadvantages.

What is Prior Probability and Posterior Probability and show how these probabilities are used in Bayes Theorem. Is there any connection between Belief Network and Bayes Theorem? Justify with an example.
When shall we use Fuzzy Logic? Explain with appropriate example. What are the major steps for developing system based on Fuzzy logic?
What is rule-based reasoning? Explain with an example. How Bayes theorem used in belief network?
What do you mean by Conceptual Dependency? Explain how knowledge is represented using scripts.

Assume the following facts: (a) Dinesh likes all kinds of food. (b) Samosa's are food. (c) Cake is food. (d) Anything anyone eats and isn't killed by is food. (e) Suresh eats peanuts and still alive. (f) Aashu eats everything suresh eats. First write all the clauses into predicates, then using resolution refutation method, prove that Dinesh likes peanuts.

In a village 1% of people have a certain genetic defect. 90% of test for gene defected people detect the defect. 9.6% of the test detect the positive result even if the person has no gene defect. If a person gets a positive test result, what are the odds they actually have the genetic defect?

What are the issues in knowledge representation? Represent the following sentences into a semantic network: Circus elephants are elephants. Elephants have head. Elephants have trunks. Heads have mouths. Elephants are animals. Animals have hearts. Circus elephants are performers. Performers have costumes. Costumes are cloths. Horatio is a circus elephant.

Explain how statistical reasoning aids in inference and reasoning in light of Bayes theorem. At a certain University, 5% of men are over 6 feet tall and 2% of women are 6 feet tall. 60% of students are female. If a student is selected at a random from among all those over six feet tall, what is the probability that the selected students is woman?
What is causal net? How does Bayes Theorem calculate the probability in a causal net? Explain with example calculation.
What is Fuzzy Logic and why is it important? Explain about Mamdani Fuzzy Inference Method with example.
What is belief network? How reasoning is done in this network?
Why do we need FOPL? State any three rules of inference. How can we make the machine with learning capacity?
Explain backward chaining with suitable example and compare with forward chaining.
What is the knowledge representation? How semantic network is used to represent knowledge?

Given premises: "Every American who sells weapons to hostile nations is a criminal. The country XYZ is enemy of America. All of its missalies in XYZ were sold by Donald, who is an American." Prove that Donald is a criminal by using FOPL based resolution refutation method.
Why CNF is required? Explain all the steps used to convert a quantified statement with suitable example.
Why semantic network and frames are important in AI? Provide examples of both with FOPL statements example.

Using resolution solve the following statements: All pompeian are Romans. All Romans were either loyal to Caesor or hated him. Everyone is loyal to someone. People only try to assassinate rulers they not loyal to. Marcus tried to assassinate Caesor. Marcus was pompeian. Find, did Marcus Caesor?

What are Frames and Semantic Net? Convert the given sentences in semantic Net: A person is a mammal. Sakti Gauchan is a person. Person has nose. Sakti Gauchan is in Nepalese team. Uniform color of Sakti Gauchan is Red/Blue.

A doctor is called to see a sick child. The doctor has prior information that 90% of sick children in that neighborhood have the flu, while the other 10% are sick with measles... A well-known symptom of measles is a rash and has probability of 0.95. However, children with flu also develop rash has probability of 0.08. Upon examining the child, the doctor finds a rash. What is the probability that the child has measles?

What is machine learning? Explain genetic algorithm (GA) along with different operators of genetic algorithm.

What do you understand by swarm intelligence? Suppose chromosomes are of the form x = a b c d e f g h with a fixed length of eight genes. Each gene can be any digit between 0 and 9. Let the fitness of individual x be calculated as: f(x)=(a+b)-(c+d)+(e+f)-(g+h) and let the initial population consist of four individuals.
Differentiate the followings: (a) Rule based learning versus Fuzzy learning. (b) Expert System versus Management Information System.
What is machine learning? Explain learning by analogy with example. List some of areas where GA can be applied.
What is Self-organizing Map (SOM)? Explain all the steps involved in SOM with suitable example.

qs.t: What is the role of activation function in ANN? Explain the McCulloch-Pitts model of a neuron with a diagram.

Below is a neural network with weights a, b, c, d, e, and f. The input are x1 and x2. The first hidden layer computes r1 = max(c.x1+e.x2, 0) and r2 = max(d.x1+f.x2, 0). The second hidden layer and third layer computes s1, s2 and y using sigmoid activation function. Suppose the network has inputs x1=-1 and x2=-1. The weight values are a=1, b=1, c=4, d=1, e=2 and f=2. Perform forward propagation. And update weight for second hidden layer for a and b.
What is perceptron? When do we need back propagation algorithm? Explain with an example.
What is neural network? Show that a single neuron cannot implement XOR gate.
What is McCulloch/Pitts neuron? Can this neuron be trained to represent EX-OR gate? Justify and propose neural network model.
Using Hebbian learning algorithm construct a neural network that behaves as an AND gate. Is hebbian learning supervised method? Justify.
What is neural network? Explain the back propagation algorithms and perceptron.
What is neural network? Explain back-propagation algorithm learning.
What is neural network? Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning.
What is a McCulloch/Pitts neural network? Explain it with reference to AND gate. Justify that it cannot be applied to Exclusive OR gate.
What do you understand by Perception? How can we design a neural network that acts as an XOR gate?

What is expert system? Create a scenario where we require expert system. List down the steps how you create this system.
Explain Hopfield network with an example.
What is a neural network? Show that a single neuron cannot implement XOR gate.
Define Hebbian learning. Use Hebbian learning algorithm to Construct Hebbian Network which perform line AND Function.
What are the applications of Expert System? Describe the Development stages of Expert System briefly.
How does machine vision help in Artificial Intelligence? Explain how back propagation algorithm helps in machine learning.
